title: Chapter 38: The Dream
content: Two years after the Choice, I have a dream.

Not a glitch. Not a fold in reality. Not the universe hiccupping and showing me sideways versions of my own life. Just a dream. The old-fashioned kind, the kind where you're asleep and your brain is doing its nightly housekeeping—filing things away, defragmenting emotional hard drives, running the occasional screensaver of nonsense. The kind that doesn't want anything from you. The kind that just is.
In the dream, I'm in the garden again.

The Garden of Forking Paths. Except it's nothing like the last time I was here—nothing like the impossible, terrifying maze of infinity that nearly cracked me open like an egg. The hedges are blooming now. Wildflowers climbing every branch—lavender and columbine and something yellow I can't name but I can smell, soft and sweet like honey left in sunlight. The air is warm, the kind of warm that doesn't press against you but holds you, like a hand on your shoulder that doesn't need to squeeze.

The paths don't fork into infinity. They wind. Gently, like walking paths in a park—curving around rose bushes and under trellises heavy with jasmine, each one leading somewhere I can almost see. A beach with white sand and blue water. A library with floor-to-ceiling shelves and a fire going. A kitchen where bread is browning in the oven. A stage where music rises and falls like breathing.
I stand at the entrance, and for the first time in this garden, I'm not afraid.
I start walking.

The ground is soft beneath my feet—not unstable, not shifting, just soft the way moss is soft, the way a forest floor gives slightly under your weight and holds you anyway. The jasmine is everywhere now, threading through the hedges, and when I brush against it, the petals release their scent in little clouds. Sweet. Almost too sweet. The way things smell in dreams.
The garden isn't a maze. It's a memory palace.

Each path I pass holds a fragment. I slow as I walk by the first one, and there it is: Julian A's office. Floor-to-ceiling windows overlooking Manhattan, the skyline sharp and glittering at dusk. His desk with the clean lines, the leather chair, the view that cost more than most people's homes. I can see him standing at the window, silhouette against the city, one hand in his pocket. He turns, half-turns, like he's heard me, and the corner of his mouth lifts.
I don't take the path.
I keep walking.

The second opening shows me Julian B's gallery. The gold-leaf painting hangs in the center of a white room, and it catches the light in a way that makes it look like the sun itself decided to be art. He's there, standing beside it, one hand reaching up like he's about to touch the frame—but he isn't. He's just looking at it, the way you look at something you made that surprised even you. His rings catch the light.
I don't take that path either.

The third one. Julian C's—Julian Gallagher's—boat at dawn. The harbor is glass, and the sky is purple turning gold, and he's standing at the rail with a cup of something steaming, looking out at the water like it owes him an answer. The wind catches his hair. He's younger in this one, maybe, or maybe I'm just seeing him the way he was before everything. Before me. Before any of this.
I don't take it.
I see them. I acknowledge them. And I keep walking.

The thing about grief—about the kind of loss that isn't death but is still loss, the mourning of lives you didn't live and people you didn't choose to leave behind but chose not to follow—the thing about it is that it doesn't disappear. It just finds a smaller room to live in. A room you don't visit as often. A room where the furniture gets rearranged every so often, and one day you open the door and realize you can stand in it without your chest caving in.

I walk, and the garden holds all of it—the could-have-beens and the almost-weres and the might-yets—and none of them reach for me. They just exist. Fragments in glass, preserved but not demanding. Beautiful in the way that things behind museum glass are beautiful: you can see them, but you don't need to touch.
At the center of the garden, there's a bench.

It's a simple thing. Wooden slats, slightly weathered, the kind of bench you'd find in any public park in any city in the world. Nothing special about it except that it's here, at the heart of everything, and sitting on it is—
Julian.

Not the Composite Julian. Not the flickering, layered, impossible overlay of every Julian from every timeline, shimmering between versions like a broken broadcast. He's just Julian. My Julian. The one who makes scones on Saturday mornings and burns the first batch every single time without fail. The one who plays guitar badly and loudly and with full conviction. The one who forgets to clean the espresso machine and leaves paint rags on the back counter and once tried to make risotto at midnight and set off the smoke alarm.

He's wearing jeans and a green sweater—the one I bought him, the one that's slightly too big because I refuse to admit he's broader across the shoulders than the size chart suggests. And there's flour in his hair. A streak of it across his temple, white against the dark, like he's been baking in his sleep.

He looks up at me, and his face does the thing it always does—the crinkle at the corners of his eyes, the slight lift of his eyebrows, the way his whole expression opens like a window someone decided to leave unlatched.
"You came back," he says.
Not accusing. Not surprised. Just noting. The way you'd note that the sun rose, or that the tide came in. A fact.
"I always come back," I say. "To you."
The words are simple. They don't shake. They don't carry the weight of a declaration or the tremor of a promise. They're just true. The sky is blue. Water is wet. I always come back to him.

He shifts on the bench, makes room, and I sit beside him. The wood is warm—not dream-warm, which would be vague and soft, but specifically warm, the way wood gets after sitting in sunlight. I can feel the grain under my palm. I can feel the absence of splinters.

He gestures at the garden—at the paths, at the fragments, at the whole impossible sprawling architecture of everything that was and wasn't. The jasmine. The wildflowers. The glimpses of beaches and libraries and kitchens and stages.
"Does it still hurt?" he asks.

He means the other timelines. The losses. The versions of him I didn't choose and the versions of me that didn't choose him. The infinite branching paths that we could have walked, didn't walk, will never walk. He means all of it.

I consider. Not because I don't know the answer, but because the answer deserves the weight of consideration. I look at the garden—the blooming hedges, the winding paths, the soft warm light that isn't coming from anywhere in particular but feels like it's coming from everywhere. I think about the girl I was two years ago, standing in the center of a different garden, watching the world fold itself into an infinite origami of possibility, and thinking she'd never survive choosing just one fold.

"It doesn't hurt anymore," I say. And I'm surprised to find that I mean it. "It just... echoes. Like a song you used to know all the words to." I pause. The jasmine is drifting toward us, and I can almost taste the sweetness at the back of my throat. "You don't forget the song. But you don't need to sing it anymore."
He nods. Slow. The flour in his hair catches the light, and I want to reach up and brush it away, but I don't. Some things are better left where they are.
"That sounds like peace," he says.
"That sounds like peace," I agree.

We sit for a while. The garden hums around us—not with the electric, reality-bending buzz of the fork, but with the ordinary hum of a place that's alive. Bees. Wind. The distant sound of water, maybe, or maybe just the sound that silence makes when it's comfortable. Julian's hand finds mine on the bench, his thumb tracing the line of my knuckles, and his skin is warm and rough and specific—the callus on his index finger from the guitar strings, the slight dryness at the tips, the familiar.
We don't say anything else. We don't need to.
I wake up.

The bedroom is gray with early morning—the particular gray of a city that hasn't decided whether it's going to rain or not, holding its breath in the liminal space between dark and light. The sheets are tangled around my legs. Julian is asleep beside me, on his back, one arm flung across his face like he's defending himself against the very concept of morning. He's snoring. Not loudly—just a soft, rhythmic rasp, like a small engine running somewhere in the middle distance.

Gatsby is wedged between us. Our cat—our ridiculous, oversized, aggressively orange cat—has inserted himself into the six-inch gap between our bodies with the confident territorialism of a creature who knows he's the actual center of this household. His fur is warm against my arm, and his purring runs like a low current through the mattress, a vibration I feel more than hear. A very hairy mediator. The only member of this household who has never once questioned a decision.
I lie still.

The dream settles over me—not urgent, not demanding, not the way the glitch-dreams used to land, like a migraine wrapped in a riddle. This one lands softly. Like a blanket. Like snow that doesn't need to be shoveled. It's still there, the garden, the bench, the jasmine, the flour in his hair. I can almost taste the sweetness at the back of my throat. But it's fading the way dreams do—kindly, gradually, leaving behind not the details but the feeling. The peace.

I think about the Garden of Forking Paths. Not my fever-dream version—the real one. The Borges story. I read it in college, before any of this happened, before I knew that reality was a thin membrane stretched over an infinite sea of might-have-beens. In the story, the garden represents everything that could have been. Every choice, every branch, every timeline where things went differently. An infinite labyrinth of forking paths, each one a world.

And here's the thing about the garden that Borges understood and that I spent two years learning: it doesn't diminish what is. It makes it more miraculous. By showing how many other ways it could have gone—how a thousand sadder endings exist alongside this one, how an infinite number of Averys are living an infinite number of lives that are worse and better and just different—it doesn't make this ending small. It makes it enormous. It was chosen. Out of everything, this. Out of everyone, him.
Every happy ending exists alongside a thousand sadder ones. That doesn't make the happy ending less meaningful.
It makes it more meaningful.
It was chosen.

Julian snorts in his sleep, mumbles something unintelligible, and rolls toward me. His arm lands across my waist, heavy and warm, and Gatsby protests with a grumpy chirp before resettling. The gray light is starting to warm now—pink creeping in at the edges of the curtains, the sky deciding, finally, to let the morning happen.
I stay for another minute. Then another. The weight of his arm. The warmth of the cat. The fading sweetness of jasmine.
Then I get up.

The kitchen is cool. I move through it by feel—muscle memory, two years of mornings, the choreography of a life that has become its own kind of dance. Kettle on. Water. The click of the stove, the blue flame stuttering to life beneath it. I reach for the tea—jasmine, because apparently my subconscious is making my beverage choices now—and the familiar ritual holds me the way the dream did. Warm. Specific. Chosen.

While the water heats, I feed the cats. Gatsby has followed me out of the bedroom, his orange bulk winding between my ankles with the strategic timing of a creature who wants to trip me into compliance. Luna appears from wherever she's been sleeping—probably on top of the refrigerator, her favored vantage point—and sits by her bowl with an expression of regal expectation. I feed them both. Gatsby eats like he's never seen food before. Luna eats like she's doing the food a favor by acknowledging it.

The kettle starts to whistle. I pour, let the tea steep, and the steam rises in a curl that catches the early light—the light that's finally, properly here now, gold and pink and spreading across the kitchen counter like spilled paint.
And there it is.
On the counter, next to the kettle. The fork keychain.

Mika gave it to me as a joke, years ago, back when the idea of choosing between realities was a thought experiment and not a crisis. A cheap metal keychain shaped like a fork—the eating kind, not the diverging-paths kind. "For when you can't decide between the salad and the pasta," she'd said, grinning. "Or the timeline and the other timeline." We'd laughed. It had been funny. A throwaway gag, a trinket from a dollar store, the kind of thing that should have gathered dust in a junk drawer and been forgotten.
It didn't gather dust.

I pick it up. Turn it over in my fingers. The metal is cool and smooth, lighter than you'd expect—tin, maybe, or whatever cheap alloy they use for novelty keychains that were never meant to mean anything. The tines are slightly bent from two years of being shoved in pockets and dropped on counters and fidgeted with during long nights when I needed something to hold. It's not elegant. It's not even well-made. The fork prongs aren't symmetrical, and there's a scratch across the handle from the time Gatsby knocked it off the table.
It's the most meaningful piece of metal I own.

Not the app. Not Ghost Fork, not the choice engine, not the shimmering interface that let me peek behind the curtain of reality—that's gone. Deleted. Collapsed. The multiverse's front door is closed, and I don't have the key anymore. What I have is this: a joke. A thing that was funny before it was profound. A fork that isn't a fork, a keychain that doesn't unlock anything, a piece of stamped metal that cost approximately one dollar and carries the weight of every choice I ever made.

That's how the best stories work, isn't it? The thing that matters isn't the magical artifact or the ancient prophecy or the world-ending power. It's the joke. The throwaway line. The trinket someone gave you because they loved you and wanted to make you laugh. It's the flour in someone's hair. It's the cat between you and the person you chose.
I slip the keychain into my pocket. The metal warms against my thigh, or maybe I'm imagining it. Doesn't matter.

The tea is ready. I drink it standing at the window, watching the street come alive—early joggers, delivery trucks, the bartender from next door unlocking the café door. My café. Our café. The one we built out of a ruined building and a wildcard choice and two years of early mornings exactly like this one.

Julian stumbles out of the bedroom. His hair is a disaster, there's a crease on his cheek from the pillow, and he's wearing my T-shirt again, the one that's too tight across his shoulders and makes him look like he's about to burst out of it like the Incredible Hulk of morning people.
"Tea?" I offer.

"Please." His voice is a gravel road. He shuffles to the counter, wraps both hands around the cup I hand him, and drinks like it's the first liquid he's encountered in a desert. There's still flour—actual flour, dream-echo flour—from his hair on the pillowcase. I'll brush it out later. Or I won't. It doesn't matter.
"You have flour in your hair," I say.
He touches his head, confused, and finds nothing. "I don't—"
"Never mind."
He shrugs. Drinks his tea. Gatsby yells at him for more food. Luna judges us both from the refrigerator.
I open the café for another day.

The keychain is in my pocket. The dream is settling into the place where dreams go when they're done. And somewhere—everywhere, maybe, in infinite branching paths that I can't see and don't need to—every version of this story is still being told. Some of them are sad. Some of them are different. Some of them end in ways I'll never know.
This one ends with a fork in my pocket and flour on the pillow and a cat who thinks he runs this household.
He does.
